content.text: Every Azure subscription has an offer type that determines its pricing, billing mechanics, quota limits, and which Azure features are available. Understanding offer types lets you select the right subscription for each workload, detect misconfigurations programmatically, and plan cost governance appropriately.

attrs.alt: Azure subscription types: Free Trial, PAYG, Dev/Test, Visual Studio, EA, MCA, CSP, Sponsored

content.text: An offer is the commercial agreement under which an Azure subscription operates. It is encoded in the subscription's `quotaId` property, which is set at creation and cannot be changed without transferring the subscription to a different billing account. The offer determines:
Pricing: list price (PAYG), negotiated discount (EA), or free credit (Free Trial, VS).
Spending limit: whether charges can accumulate beyond a credit balance.
SLA eligibility: Dev/Test subscriptions carry no SLA; production subscriptions inherit the service SLA.
Feature availability: some features (e.g., Entra ID PIM, certain Marketplace offers) require specific subscription types.
Quota defaults: initial VM vCPU quotas and other soft limits are offer-dependent.

# Inspect the offer type of the active subscription
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{Name:displayName, QuotaId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId, SpendingLimit:subscriptionPolicies.spendingLimit, State:state}'

# List all subscriptions and their offer types
for SUB in $(az account list --all --query '[].id' -o tsv); do
  INFO=$(az rest --method GET \
    --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${SUB}?api-version=2022-12-01" \
    --query '{name:displayName, quota:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId}' -o json)
  echo $INFO
done

The Free Trial offer (quotaId: FreeTrial) is created when signing up via the Azure Free Account path. It includes a $200 credit valid for 30 days and a spending limit that prevents charges once the credit is exhausted.
Active by default; suspends resources at $0 credit
Full SLAs apply on covered services
Disable spending limit → converts to PAYG (one-way)

# Check if spending limit is active on a Free Trial sub
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.spendingLimit'
# Returns 'On' (active) or 'Off' (disabled/upgraded to PAYG)

# Upgrade is via Azure portal: Subscriptions -> Upgrade
# After upgrade, quotaId changes to PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01

PAYG (quotaId: PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01) is the standard individual subscription. Usage is billed monthly at list prices to a payment method. No commitment, no minimum spend, and no volume discounts — but Azure Reservations and Savings Plans can be purchased to reduce costs.
Most flexible: create resources immediately, cancel at any time.
Full SLA on all covered services.
Can purchase Azure Reservations and Savings Plans to reduce costs by 40–72%.
Dev/Test pricing not available — requires a separate Dev/Test subscription.
Default initial vCPU quota: 20 per region (requestable increase).

# Confirm PAYG offer type
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId'
# Expected: 'PayAsYouGo_2014-09-01'

# Purchase a 1-year Reservation to save ~40% on a Standard_D2s_v5 VM
az reservations reservation-order calculate \
  --applied-scope-type Single \
  --applied-scopes '[/subscriptions/<sub-id>]' \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --term P1Y \
  --billing-plan Upfront \
  --quantity 1 \
  --sku-name Standard_D2s_v5 \
  --location eastus \
  --query '{EstimatedSavings:properties.savingsAmount, Price:properties.netTotal}'

Dev/Test subscriptions offer reduced pricing on Windows Server and SQL Server VMs for active Visual Studio subscribers. They are licensed only for development and testing workloads — production use violates the agreement and may result in audit findings.

# Create a Dev/Test subscription under MCA
az account create \
  --display-name 'engineering-devtest' \
  --billing-account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --billing-profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section-id> \
  --workload DevTest

# Verify workload type
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{QuotaId:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId}'

# Apply Azure Hybrid Benefit on a VM in Dev/Test sub (further savings)
az vm update \
  --resource-group rg-devtest \
  --name vm-dev-01 \
  --license-type Windows_Server

# Audit: find all VMs in Dev/Test subs running Windows without Hybrid Benefit
az vm list \
  --query '[?storageProfile.osDisk.osType==`Windows` && licenseType!=`Windows_Server`].{Name:name,RG:resourceGroup}' \
  -o table

Dev/Test subscriptions have no SLA. Microsoft explicitly does not guarantee uptime for resources in Dev/Test subscriptions. Running customer-facing services or databases with production data in a Dev/Test subscription is a licensing violation and leaves you without recourse if there is an outage.
Active Visual Studio (formerly MSDN) subscribers receive a monthly Azure credit as part of their subscription benefit. The credit size depends on the VS subscription tier and resets monthly.

Credits reset at the start of each calendar month and do not roll over. If a month's credit is exhausted, Azure suspends resources unless the subscriber enables Pay-As-You-Go billing on the subscription. VS subscriber benefit subscriptions also get Dev/Test pricing on Windows VMs.

# Check remaining credit balance (VS benefit subscription)
# Via portal: Cost Management -> Credit balance
# Via CLI: list usage for the current month
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query 'sum([].pretaxCost)' -o tsv

# VS benefit subscriptions reset on the 1st; check quotaId
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies'

EA subscriptions are created under an EA enrollment and inherit the negotiated pricing and governance structure of the enrollment. Multiple subscriptions can exist under one enrollment, organized by department and account.

# Create a new EA subscription (requires EA Account Owner role)
az account create \
  --display-name 'platform-production' \
  --enrollment-account-name <enrollment-account-object-id> \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0017P

# List all EA subscriptions in an enrollment account
az billing subscription list \
  --account-name <ea-billing-account-id> \
  --query '[?contains(subscriptionBillingStatus,`Active`)].{Name:displayName,Id:subscriptionId}' \
  -o table

# Move a subscription to a different EA department
az billing subscription move \
  --account-name <ea-billing-account-id> \
  --name <subscription-billing-name> \
  --destination-invoice-section-id /providers/Microsoft.Billing/billingAccounts/<ea>/billingProfiles/<profile>/invoiceSections/<section>
MCA subscriptions are linked to a Billing Profile → Invoice Section hierarchy. They support the same workload types as PAYG and EA but with a modern billing API and no minimum commitment.

# Create a Production subscription under MCA
az account create \
  --display-name 'production-platform' \
  --billing-account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --billing-profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  --invoice-section-name <invoice-section-id> \
  --workload Production

# List invoice sections to find valid targets for subscription creation
az billing invoice section list \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --profile-name <billing-profile-id> \
  -o table

# Rename an MCA subscription
az billing subscription update \
  --account-name <billing-account-id> \
  --name <subscription-billing-name> \
  --display-name 'production-platform-v2'
Azure for Students and Sponsored Subscriptions
Microsoft provides zero-credit-card subscriptions for verified students and sponsored subscriptions for partners, hackathons, and academic programs.

# Azure for Students — no card required, .edu email verified via Azure portal
# After signup, verify the subscription type
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId'
# Expected: 'AzureForStudents_2018-01-01'

# Azure Pass subscriptions are provided as a redemption code
# Redeemed at: microsoftazurepass.com
# quotaId after redemption starts with 'AzurePass'
Subscription Quotas and Limits by Offer Type

Default resource quotas (soft limits) vary by subscription offer type. PAYG subscriptions start with conservative defaults; EA and MCA subscriptions can have higher defaults negotiated. All quotas can be increased by submitting a quota increase request.

# Check current VM vCPU quota for the active subscription/region
az vm list-usage --location eastus \
  --query '[?name.value==`cores`].{Limit:limit,Used:currentValue}'

# Request a quota increase (standard vCPUs in eastus)
az support tickets create \
  --ticket-name 'quota-increase-vcpu-eastus' \
  --title 'Request vCPU quota increase to 100 in East US' \
  --problem-classification '/providers/Microsoft.Support/services/quota-increase/problemClassifications/sql-resource-manager' \
  --severity minimal \
  --contact-first-name 'Alice' \
  --contact-last-name 'Smith' \
  --contact-email alice@company.com \
  --contact-country US \
  --contact-preferred-support-language en-US \
  --description 'Need 100 vCPUs in eastus for Standard_D series VMs for production workload'

# Check all quotas that are near their limit (>80% utilized)
az vm list-usage --location eastus \
  --query '[?currentValue > `0`].{Name:name.localizedValue, Used:currentValue, Limit:limit, Pct:to_string(currentValue)}' \
  -o table
Subscription Naming and Tagging Best Practices

Subscription display names and tags are the primary way humans and billing tools identify subscriptions. Establishing a naming convention before creating subscriptions prevents confusion as the estate grows.

# Rename a subscription (display name only — ID is immutable)
az account alias create \
  --name 'sub-platform-prod-001' \
  --billing-scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>

# Update subscription display name via management REST API
az rest --method PUT \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/aliases/<alias>?api-version=2021-10-01' \
  --body '{"properties":{"displayName":"platform-production-eastus"}}'

# Tag a subscription for cost allocation (at subscription resource scope)
az tag create \
  --resource-id /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCenter=12345 Owner=platform-team Application=core

# List all subscriptions with their tags
az account list --all \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Id:id}' -o table

Large organizations use automation to provision subscriptions on-demand as part of a Landing Zone vending machine pattern. The Azure CLI, REST API, and Bicep all support programmatic subscription creation under EA or MCA billing accounts.

# Prerequisites: Enrollment Account Owner role on the EA enrollment account
# Step 1: Get the enrollment account object ID
az billing enrollment-account list \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Principal:principalName}' \
  -o table

# Step 2: Create the subscription
az account create \
  --display-name 'workload-prod-eastus' \
  --enrollment-account-name <enrollment-account-object-id> \
  --offer-type MS-AZR-0017P

# Offer type codes:
# MS-AZR-0017P = EA Production
# MS-AZR-0148P = EA Dev/Test

# Step 3: Retrieve the new subscription ID
NEW_SUB=$(az account list --query '[?name==`workload-prod-eastus`].id' -o tsv)
echo "New subscription: $NEW_SUB"

# Step 4: Move subscription into a management group
az account management-group subscription add \
  --name mg-production \
  --subscription $NEW_SUB

# Deploy the vending Bicep at tenant scope
az deployment tenant create \
  --template-file subscription-vend.bicep \
  --location eastus \
  --parameters \
    billingAccountName=<billing-account-id> \
    billingProfileName=<billing-profile-id> \
    invoiceSectionName=<invoice-section-id> \
    subscriptionDisplayName=workload-prod-eastus \
    managementGroupId=mg-production

# Get the output subscription ID
az deployment tenant show \
  --name subscription-vend \
  --query properties.outputs.subscriptionId.value -o tsv
Subscriptions pass through states during their lifecycle. Understanding these states — and the data retention rules at each stage — prevents accidental data loss during decommissioning.
Data persists for 30 days past due
Data retained for 90 days after deletion

# Check subscription state
az account show --query 'state' -o tsv

# Cancel (delete) a subscription via REST API
# WARNING: This suspends all resources. You have 90 days to reactivate.
az rest --method DELETE \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/cancel?api-version=2021-10-01'

# Reactivate a cancelled subscription within the 90-day window
az rest --method POST \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/enable?api-version=2021-10-01'

# Rename a subscription before decommissioning (helps audit trails)
az rest --method PUT \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>/providers/Microsoft.Subscription/aliases/decommission-rename?api-version=2021-10-01' \
  --body '{"properties":{"displayName":"DECOMMISSIONED-old-workload-2024","workload":"Production"}}'

# List all subscriptions including disabled ones
az account list --all \
  --query '[].{Name:name, State:state, Id:id}' \
  -o table

When a subscription enters Deleted state, all resources are deallocated but not immediately destroyed. You have 90 days to reactivate before Microsoft permanently deletes the resources. Always export critical data and delete resources explicitly before cancelling a subscription to avoid the 90-day holding period.
Offer Type Detection in Automation Scripts

Scripts that interact with billing APIs often need to branch based on the subscription offer type, since EA, MCA, CSP, and PAYG expose different billing endpoints. The following patterns implement robust offer detection.

#!/bin/bash
# detect-offer-type.sh — detect subscription type and call appropriate billing API

SUB_ID=${1:-$(az account show --query id -o tsv)}

QUOTA=$(az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/${SUB_ID}?api-version=2022-12-01" \
  --query 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId' -o tsv)

echo "Subscription: $SUB_ID"
echo "Offer (quotaId): $QUOTA"

if [[ $QUOTA == FreeTrial* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Free Trial'
  echo 'Billing: Spending limit active; no billing API calls needed'
elif [[ $QUOTA == PayAsYouGo* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Pay-As-You-Go'
  az consumption usage list --start-date $(date +%Y-%m-01) --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
    --query 'sum([].pretaxCost)' -o tsv
elif [[ $QUOTA == EnterpriseAgreement* || $QUOTA == EA_* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Enterprise Agreement'
  echo 'Use ea.azure.com for enrollment-level cost reports'
elif [[ $QUOTA == MCA_* || $QUOTA == MCA* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Microsoft Customer Agreement'
  az billing subscription list --account-name $(az billing account list --query '[0].name' -o tsv) -o table 2>/dev/null
elif [[ $QUOTA == CSPARM* || $QUOTA == CSP* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: CSP - contact partner for billing details'
elif [[ $QUOTA == MSDN* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Visual Studio subscriber benefit'
elif [[ $QUOTA == AzureForStudents* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Azure for Students'
elif [[ $QUOTA == AzurePass* ]]; then
  echo 'Type: Azure Pass (sponsored)'
else
  echo "Type: Unknown (quotaId: $QUOTA)"
fi

# Python: determine offer type and apply appropriate cost query
import subprocess, json

def get_offer_type(sub_id: str) -> str:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ['az', 'rest', '--method', 'GET',
         '--url', f'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/{sub_id}?api-version=2022-12-01',
         '--query', 'subscriptionPolicies.quotaId', '-o', 'tsv'],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    return result.stdout.strip()

OFFER_MAP = {
    'FreeTrial': 'free_trial',
    'PayAsYouGo': 'payg',
    'MSDN': 'visual_studio',
    'MSDNDevTest': 'devtest',
    'EnterpriseAgreement': 'ea',
    'EA_': 'ea',
    'MCA': 'mca',
    'CSPARM': 'csp',
    'AzureForStudents': 'students',
    'AzurePass': 'sponsored',
}

def classify_offer(quota_id: str) -> str:
    for prefix, category in OFFER_MAP.items():
        if quota_id.startswith(prefix):
            return category
    return 'unknown'

# Example usage
import subprocess
sub_id = subprocess.run(['az', 'account', 'show', '--query', 'id', '-o', 'tsv'],
                        capture_output=True, text=True).stdout.strip()
quota = get_offer_type(sub_id)
print(f'Subscription {sub_id}: {quota} -> {classify_offer(quota)}')

Azure subscriptions come in several offer types distinguished by the `quotaId` property: Free Trial ($200 credit, 30 days), Pay-As-You-Go (list price, no commit), Dev/Test (reduced Windows pricing for VS subscribers, no SLA), Visual Studio (monthly credit, Dev/Test pricing), Enterprise Agreement (negotiated discounts, $100K/year commit), MCA (modern billing hierarchy, no minimum), and CSP/Sponsored (partner or Microsoft-provided). The offer type is set at creation and changes only through subscription transfer. Always verify the `quotaId` programmatically before applying billing-specific automation, and request quota increases early for production workloads to avoid capacity surprises.
